bootstrap.author: offsidewinger
bootstrap.title: Got deked so badly last night that I bought the kid a beer after
bootstrap.body:
Summer league, first shift of the third. Their winger is maybe 20, I've got skate socks older than him. He comes down the wall, I line him up for the big defensive stand of my season, and he slips it between my legs, picks it up behind me, and scores while I'm still checking my pockets for the puck.

My bench gave me a standing ovation. I bought him a beer in the parking lot because that's the rule. He called me sir, which honestly hurt worse than the goal.

comments.author: elbows_up
comments.body: The beer is mandatory, the sir is a war crime. Proper etiquette says he owes YOU one for the assist.

replies.author: offsidewinger
replies.body: An assist is generous but I'll take it. Stats are stats.

comments.author: penaltyboxphil
comments.body: Happens every summer. Some junior kid keeping his hands warm. Next shift you angle him to the boards and let the boards do the talking. Legally, I mean.

comments.author: goalie_mom_deb
comments.body: My son does this to grown men at his summer skates and I want you to know they all react exactly like you did — laughing in the moment, quietly doing extra squats for a week after. The beer rule is why I love this sport.

comments.author: zamboni_dreams
comments.body: I see this from the bay door a few times a week. For what it's worth, from ice level everyone getting deked looks the same: one confident stride, then a small prayer.

bootstrap.author: penaltyboxphil
bootstrap.title: One shift. Game 7 overtime. Any player, any era. Who goes over the boards?
bootstrap.body:
Simple question. One shift to end it all.

My answer is Orr. The man won a final in overtime and was airborne when the puck went in. Best player on the ice every night he played, both ends, didn't matter. Convince me otherwise.

comments.author: portage_and_main
comments.body: Gretzky, my friend, and it's not close. The man has more career assists than anyone else has points. One shift means one chance, and nobody in history made more out of one chance than 99.

replies.author: penaltyboxphil
replies.body: He needs room to work. It's overtime, everyone's collapsed to the slot, nobody's giving him the office behind the net. Orr creates his own space. Fair pick though.

comments.author: elbows_up
comments.body: Lemieux once scored five goals five different ways in a single game — even strength, power play, shorthanded, penalty shot, empty net. That's not a player, that's a settings menu. He goes over the boards.

comments.author: offsidewinger
comments.body: As a defenceman I'm contractually obligated to say Orr. As a guy who watched the 2010 golden goal standing on a chair in a packed bar, some part of me will always answer Crosby.

comments.author: goalie_mom_deb
comments.body: Everyone's picking shooters. That shift starts in your own end more often than not. Give me prime Patrick Roy in the net first, and then we can argue about who's up front.

replies.author: elbows_up
replies.body: Goalies don't take shifts, Deb. They serve sentences.

comments.author: crokinole_king
comments.body: Paul Henderson scored the winner in games six, seven and eight of the '72 Summit Series. Three straight must-win games. If everything's on the line, history says you send Henderson over the boards and look away.

bootstrap.author: goalie_mom_deb
bootstrap.title: Cleaned out the tournament trunk today. Found three summers.
bootstrap.body:
July tournament season means the trunk never really gets unpacked, but today I committed. Full inventory: one set of goalie pads (current), one blocker (retired, sentimental), four camp chairs, a cooler containing one juice box of unknown vintage, $3.85 in loose change, and a trophy from two years ago that never made it into the house.

Put everything back except the juice box. Season's not over.

comments.author: portage_and_main
comments.body: The trophy that lives in the trunk is a national tradition. Ours rode around for a full year — my daughter said it liked the car. Who was I to argue.

comments.author: offsidewinger
comments.body: My trunk is one bag, one stick, and a smell I've stopped apologizing for. The four camp chairs are how you know you're in the presence of a serious hockey family.

replies.author: goalie_mom_deb
replies.body: The chairs are load-bearing. You try standing through a 7 a.m. game on a July Sunday.

comments.author: zamboni_dreams
comments.body: I open the rink at 5:30 in tournament season. The trunk people are always first in the lot — coffee in hand, chairs on shoulders. Quiet respect.

comments.author: elbows_up
comments.body: $3.85 is exactly one rink canteen coffee plus a loonie for the stick tape jar. The trunk provides.

bootstrap.author: zamboni_dreams
bootstrap.title: Things people yell at me from the glass, a running list
bootstrap.body:
Ten years driving the machine. In order of frequency: you missed a spot (daily, I did not), can I get a ride (weekly, no, insurance), do a lap for me (sure, that's literally the job), and one marriage proposal (flattered, declined, she was clearly yelling it at the machine and not at me).

The ice doesn't care about any of it. Smooth ice, smooth mind.

comments.author: elbows_up
comments.body: smooth ice smooth mind is going on the dressing room wall. honest question though: what's the actual best part of the job?

replies.author: zamboni_dreams
replies.body: The last lap. Whole sheet behind you like glass, building gone quiet, lights sitting on the fresh water. Ten years in and I still take one extra second at the gate to look at it.

comments.author: offsidewinger
comments.body: I have absolutely yelled you missed a spot. In my defence I was twelve. In further defence I did it again last winter.

replies.author: zamboni_dreams
replies.body: We know. We always know. There's a mental list, and the ice is slightly slower on your side. That's a joke. Probably.

comments.author: goalie_mom_deb
comments.body: The little kids pressed against the glass waving like you're driving a parade float — do you wave back? My daughter is convinced the driver sees her specifically.

replies.author: zamboni_dreams
replies.body: Every driver I know waves back. She's right. We see her specifically.

comments.author: penaltyboxphil
comments.body: Best seat in the building. Nobody boos the zamboni. Nobody yells at the zamboni driver about his contract. The machine figured something out the rest of us haven't.

bootstrap.author: elbows_up
bootstrap.title: Unwritten rules of summer shinny. I'll start.
bootstrap.body:
No slapshots, ever, I don't care how open you are. Pass to the kid, and if the kid scores it counts double in everyone's heart. Goals don't count if the goalie was fixing his water bottle. And everybody knows exactly what a big celly costs you socially in a no-contact game.

Whoever brings the nets makes the teams. That one's constitutional. Add yours.

comments.author: penaltyboxphil
comments.body: No going top shelf on a new guy until you've seen him play a full shift. And whoever scores the winner carries the nets back. Keeps the snipers humble.

comments.author: offsidewinger
comments.body: You call your own penalties, which in my case means announcing my hooking in real time like a self-narrating documentary.

replies.author: elbows_up
replies.body: The honour system only works because your guilt is louder than any ref.

comments.author: portage_and_main
comments.body: Prairie amendment: if the ball or puck leaves the surface for the tall grass, the shooter fetches it, no matter whose team touched it last. This law predates all of us.

comments.author: codfather
comments.body: Same rules at the rink back home, b'y, plus one more: whoever complains about the teams gets traded. Instantly, and alone.

bootstrap.author: portage_and_main
bootstrap.title: Opened the garage for the first time in weeks and my hockey bag ambushed me. It's July. It's still in game shape.
bootstrap.body:
The bag hasn't seen action since April and it's out there holding its ground like it's game seven. Gave everything the summer airing on the back fence, which is a Winnipeg tradition my neighbours have learned to schedule their barbecues around.

Science can land a rover on Mars but it cannot make hockey gear surrender. Some things are simply bigger than us.

comments.author: offsidewinger
comments.body: I bought one of those drying racks with the little fans, plus a spray bottle of vinegar mix. My gear now smells like a salad having a rough season, which I'm calling progress.

comments.author: zamboni_dreams
comments.body: The rink holds onto it in summer too. Fifty years of hockey in the walls. You get used to it, then you get fond of it, then you start to worry about yourself.

comments.author: elbows_up
comments.body: the smell is a pilot light. it never fully goes out, it just waits for the season

replies.author: portage_and_main
replies.body: The pilot light theory explains a great deal. Somewhere deep in that bag, a small flame of pure 1996 is still burning.

comments.author: goalie_mom_deb
comments.body: Goalie gear laughs at your fence. We have a dedicated corner of the garage, a box fan on a timer, and an understanding with the neighbours that took years of diplomacy.

drip.author: elbows_up
drip.title: The guy who shows up to pickup in brand-new top-of-the-line everything is either the best skater on the ice or the worst. There is no third option.

comments.author: offsidewinger
comments.body: I showed up to a new group in new everything once, because my old gear all died the same month. Spent the whole skate overcompensating. Can confirm I was not the best skater on the ice.

replies.author: elbows_up
replies.body: new gear is a promise you make in front of witnesses

comments.author: penaltyboxphil
comments.body: Corollary: the best player at any pickup is wearing a helmet older than the rink itself. Every league, every province. It's a law.

comments.author: goalie_mom_deb
comments.body: The goalie version is scarier. Brand new pads means either they're sponsored or it's their third skate ever, and your shooters are about to learn patience either way.

drip.author: goalie_mom_deb
drip.title: He let in seven this weekend and came off the ice grinning
drip.body:
Summer tournament, tough pool. Seven got past my son and I braced for the quiet car ride. Instead he comes off beaming, because on one of the saves — the ONE save, full-stretch glove, the kind he practises on the trampoline all summer — the whole rink made that sound.

Seven goals, one memory. He's still doing the glove motion at breakfast. Worth it.

comments.author: elbows_up
comments.body: the whole rink making that sound is the actual currency of the game. the score is just bookkeeping

comments.author: portage_and_main
comments.body: That's the sport, right there. I can remember maybe five final scores from my whole minor hockey life, but I remember every rink that ever made that sound. Good on him.

comments.author: zamboni_dreams
comments.body: You can hear that sound from the zamboni bay through two sets of doors. We always know when a goalie is having their moment. Tell him the machine room noticed.

replies.author: goalie_mom_deb
replies.body: He is going to lose his mind that the machine room noticed. That's better than a trophy.

drip.author: offsidewinger
drip.title: Started dryland training so fall me can be slightly less of a disappointment to winter me
drip.body:
Every July I make the same promise: this is the year I show up in September with legs. Bought a skipping rope, dusted off the squat rack, did lunges until my dignity gave out somewhere around the mailbox.

Realistically this lasts three weeks. But they're going to be three tremendous weeks.

comments.author: kettlebellkate
comments.body: Three weeks is three more than zero, and today is always the second-best time to start. One tip: skip the hero workout on day one. Sore quads quit faster than lazy ones.

replies.author: offsidewinger
replies.body: Too late on the hero workout. Currently descending stairs sideways like a crab with regrets.

comments.author: penaltyboxphil
comments.body: Skating legs only come from skating. Find a summer stick-and-puck. But squats never hurt anybody who did them right, so carry on.

comments.author: elbows_up
comments.body: the annual july promise is a sacred rite. see you at the september skate where we all pretend we kept it

drip.author: portage_and_main
drip.title: Settle it once and for all: the best hockey movie ever made
drip.body:
Watched one of the classics at the lake this weekend — the air conditioning was the real draw — and the argument outlasted the movie by a good hour. So let's have it out properly.

My answer hasn't moved since my dad first sat me down in front of it: Slap Shot. Everything else is playing for second.

comments.author: offsidewinger
comments.body: Slap Shot is the correct answer, but the Mighty Ducks movies built more actual hockey players than any development program in history. The knucklepuck alone sent a million kids into the driveway. That has to count for something.

comments.author: goalie_mom_deb
comments.body: All the rink parents say Slap Shot, and then we put the Mighty Ducks on at every tournament hotel and recite it word for word. Both things are true.

replies.author: portage_and_main
replies.body: The tournament hotel screening is a sanctioned cultural ceremony. No notes.

comments.author: elbows_up
comments.body: casting a sincere vote for Goon. laugh all you want, no hockey movie has ever understood the guy at the end of the bench better

comments.author: penaltyboxphil
comments.body: Slap Shot. Not close. Next question.

drip.author: penaltyboxphil
drip.title: The shootout is a skills competition wearing a game's uniform
drip.body:
Sixty-five minutes of team hockey and then we settle it with a contest. Never sat right with me. Three-on-three overtime is the best rule change of my lifetime — it's chaos, it's hockey, and somebody earns it.

Beer league does it best anyway: tie game, everybody shakes hands, nobody's insulted.

comments.author: elbows_up
comments.body: 3 on 3 is the closest the pros ever get to shinny and you can tell they love it. the ice opens up and everyone's twelve years old again

comments.author: portage_and_main
comments.body: I'll push back gently: a shootout in the building is electric. Whole rink on its feet for one shooter and one goalie. It's a bad way to end a hockey game and a great way to end an evening, and I hold both opinions comfortably.

replies.author: penaltyboxphil
replies.body: Fair. It's great theatre. So is a movie. I came for a game.

comments.author: goalie_mom_deb
comments.body: Goalie parent perspective: the shootout is a horror film where my kid is the only one on screen. Six skaters lose a game and one child in oversized pads wears it. I'll take the tie and the handshakes, thanks.

drip.author: zamboni_dreams
drip.title: Why we flood with hot water (the answer surprises people)
drip.body:
Most rinks flood with water around 60 degrees. Sounds backwards, but hot water holds less dissolved air, so it settles into the grooves and freezes into denser, clearer, harder ice — and it bonds better with the layer underneath. Cold water traps little bubbles and you get soft, chippy ice.

Every ice maker I've worked with treats the water like a chef treats stock. We have opinions about water. Many opinions.

comments.author: elbows_up
comments.body: ten years of yelling trivia at intermission and I never knew this. the machine has depths

comments.author: goalie_mom_deb
comments.body: Is this why the ice feels like mashed potatoes by the last game of a tournament day? My son swears the 6 p.m. game is a different sport than the 8 a.m. one.

replies.author: zamboni_dreams
replies.body: That's exactly it. A full day of skates chews up the surface, and the building warms up with all those bodies in it. The last game of the day is played on the rink's tired legs, same as everyone else's.

comments.author: offsidewinger
comments.body: opinions about water like a chef has about stock is the most quietly intimidating sentence anyone has posted in this room

drip.author: offsidewinger
drip.title: We lost the summer league final and it was still the best night of the year
drip.body:
Lost 4-2. I was on the ice for three of theirs, which at this point is a personal brand. Then the whole league ended up in the parking lot with lawn chairs and a couple of coolers until the rink manager kicked us out around midnight, and honestly? Wouldn't trade it.

Banners fade. Parking lot nights are forever.

comments.author: elbows_up
comments.body: parking lot nights are the actual playoffs and everyone knows it. condolences on the three, congratulations on the forever

comments.author: goalie_mom_deb
comments.body: Banners fade, parking lot nights are forever might be the truest thing ever posted in this room. I hope your goalie got a beer too — four against in a final is a lonely stat line.

replies.author: offsidewinger
replies.body: First beer went to the goalie, as is law. He earned it, and also he has seen things. Specifically me, screening him on two of the four.

comments.author: portage_and_main
comments.body: That's the whole sport in one post, my friend. The score is for the record book, the lot is for the memory. See you at puck drop in the fall.

comments.author: zamboni_dreams
comments.body: For the record, no rink manager enjoys ending parking lot night. We just have to lock the gate eventually. Some of us sit in the office an extra twenty minutes to let it breathe.

late_comments.author: portage_and_main
late_comments.body: Late to this thread, but I need you to know the same kid exists in Winnipeg. Every summer league has one. I'm fairly sure the league office issues them along with the pucks.

late_comments.author: zamboni_dreams
late_comments.body: Reading this from the machine room: I've made ice for future pros and for Tuesday night beer leaguers, and the ice can't tell the difference. But if it's one shift for everything, you send whoever wants it most. That's usually not the most famous name on the list.

late_comments.author: chinook_wind
late_comments.body: Found this thread late and immediately went and checked my own trunk. Calgary edition: two camp chairs, a snow brush in July, and one puck of completely unknown origin. The prairie trunk provides.

late_comments.author: portage_and_main
late_comments.body: Late addition to the list, from a guy who grew up rinkside in Winnipeg: the old timers who give you a single nod per flood. Highest honour the building can bestow, and they know it.

late_comments.author: penaltyboxphil
late_comments.body: Two minutes for airing it out upwind of the neighbours. Take your seat, serve your time.
